title: The Tell-Tale Heart
summary: Guilt makes a man hear a heart. Centers on Spirit,  human attempt to understand the soul, afterlife, and the unseen forces that govern existence..
full_text:
True! Nervous, very, very dreadfully nervous I had been and am, but why will you say that I am mad? The disease had sharpened my senses, not destroyed, not dulled them. Above all was the sense of hearing acute. I heard all things in the heaven and in the earth. I heard many things in hell. How then am I mad? Hearken, and observe how healthily, how calmly I can tell you the whole story.

It is impossible to say how first the idea entered my brain, but once conceived, it haunted me day and night. Object there was none. Passion there was none. I loved the old man. He had never wronged me, never given me insult. For his gold I had no desire. I think it was his eye—yes, it was this! One of his eyes resembled that of a vulture, a pale blue eye with a film over it. Whenever it fell upon me, my blood ran cold, and so by degrees, very gradually, I made up my mind to take the life of the old man and thus rid myself of the eye forever.

Now this is the point: you fancy me mad. Madmen know nothing. But you should have seen me. You should have seen how wisely I proceeded, with what caution, with what foresight, with what dissimulation I went to work! I was never kinder to the old man than during the whole week before I killed him. And every night, about midnight, I turned the latch of his door and opened it—oh, so gently! And then, when I had made an opening sufficient for my head, I put in a dark lantern, all closed, so that no light shone out, and then I thrust in my head. Oh, you would have laughed to see how cunningly I thrust it in! I moved it slowly, very, very slowly, so that I might not disturb the old man's sleep. It took me an hour to place my whole head within the opening so far that I could see him as he lay upon his bed. Ha—would a madman have been so wise as this? And then, when my head was well in the room, I undid the lantern cautiously—oh, so cautiously, cautiously—for the hinges creaked. I undid it just so much that a single thin ray fell upon the vulture eye. And this I did for seven long nights, every night just at midnight, but I found the eye always closed, and so it was impossible to do the work, for it was not the old man who vexed me but his Evil Eye.

But upon the eighth night, I was more cautious than usual in opening the door. The watch's minute hand moved more quickly than mine. Before I could ascertain my position, the old man sprang up in bed, crying out, "Who's there?" I kept quite still and said nothing. For a whole hour I did not move a muscle, and in the meantime I did not hear him lie down. He was still sitting up in bed, listening. Presently I heard a slight groan, and I knew it was the groan of mortal terror. It was not a groan of pain or of grief—oh, no—it was the low, stifled sound that arises from the bottom of the soul when overcharged with awe. I knew the sound well. I pitied him, although I chuckled at heart. I knew that he had been lying awake, his fears growing upon him, telling himself it was nothing but the wind in the chimney, a mouse crossing the floor, a cricket's chirp. But all in vain, because Death, in approaching him, had stalked with his black shadow before him and enveloped the victim. When I had waited a long time, very patiently, I resolved to open the lantern. I opened it until a single dim ray, like the thread of the spider, shot out and fell upon the vulture eye. It was open—wide, wide open—and I grew furious as I gazed upon it. I saw it with perfect distinctness, all a dull blue with a hideous veil over it that chilled the very marrow in my bones.

And now I say, there came to my ears a low, dull, quick sound, such as a watch makes when enveloped in cotton. I knew that sound well, too. It was the beating of the old man's heart. It increased my fury, as the beating of a drum stimulates the soldier into courage. But even yet I refrained and kept still. The hellish tattoo of the heart increased. It grew quicker and quicker, louder and louder every instant. The old man's terror must have been extreme. It grew louder, I say, louder every moment! At the dead hour of the night, amid the dreadful silence of that old house, so strange a noise excited me to uncontrollable terror. But the beating grew louder, louder! And now a new anxiety seized me—the sound would be heard by a neighbor! The old man's hour had come! With a loud yell, I threw open the lantern and leaped into the room. He shrieked once—once only. In an instant I dragged him to the floor and pulled the heavy bed over him. I then smiled gaily to find the deed so far done. But for many minutes, the heart beat on with a muffled sound. At length it ceased. The old man was dead. I removed the bed and examined the corpse. He was stone dead. His eye would trouble me no more.

If still you think me mad, you will think so no longer when I describe the wise precautions I took for the concealment of the body. I worked hastily but in silence. I dismembered the corpse. I cut off the head and the arms and the legs. I took up three planks from the flooring and deposited all between the scantlings. I then replaced the boards so cleverly that no human eye could detect anything wrong. I then took my brush and washed the whole room, leaving not a spot of blood. When I had finished, I felt that I had committed the perfect crime. But the officers from the police arrived, summoned by a neighbor who had heard a shriek. I welcomed them with a confident smile and invited them to search. I led them through the entire house, showing them every corner. I brought chairs into the room where the body lay concealed, and I set them down on the very spot. My manner was so calm that they were satisfied. We sat and talked. I was singularly at ease. But soon I began to hear a sound—a low, dull, quick sound, much such a sound as a watch makes when enveloped in cotton. I grew pale. I talked more loudly. The sound increased. I could bear those hypocritical smiles no longer. "Villains!" I shrieked. "I admit the deed! Tear up the planks! Here, here! It is the beating of his hideous heart!"

title: The Legend of Sleepy Hollow
summary: In the year 1790, nestled along the eastern shore of the Hudson River in New York, there lay a secluded glen known as Sleepy Hollow.
full_text:
In the year 1790, nestled along the eastern shore of the Hudson River in New York, there lay a secluded glen known as Sleepy Hollow. This quiet valley, part of the Dutch settlement of Tarrytown, was a place of drowsy, dreamy influences that seemed to pervade the very atmosphere. The inhabitants of this peaceful region were known for their superstitious natures, and the whole neighborhood abounded with local tales, haunted spots, and twilight superstitions. Ghosts walked among them, and the air itself was thick with whispered stories of witchcraft and spectral appearances.

It was into this strange and captivating landscape that a Yankee schoolteacher named Ichabod Crane arrived from Connecticut. Ichabod was a curious figure: tall and exceedingly thin, with narrow shoulders, long arms and legs, hands that dangled a mile out of his sleeves, feet that might have served as shovels, and a whole frame most loosely hung together. His head was small and flat at top, with huge ears, large glassy green eyes, and a long snipe nose, so that it looked like a weathercock perched upon his spindle neck to tell which way the wind blew. To see him striding along a hillside on a windy day, with his clothes fluttering about him, one might have mistaken him for some scarecrow eloped from a cornfield.

Despite his comical appearance, Ichabod was a man of considerable ambition. He considered himself a scholar, having read Cotton Mather's History of Witchcraft from cover to cover, and his imagination was thoroughly stocked with tales of the supernatural. He lodged with the families of his students, spending a week at each house, and made himself a welcome guest through his skills at teaching singing and his willingness to help with small tasks. But Ichabod's heart was set on higher things. He had cast his eyes upon Katrina Van Tassel, the only daughter of a wealthy Dutch farmer named Baltus Van Tassel. Katrina was a plump, rosy-cheeked beauty of eighteen, and more importantly to Ichabod, she was the sole heir to her father's vast fortune.

Ichabod's rival for Katrina's affections was Abraham Van Brunt, better known as Brom Bones. Brom was a burly, swaggering young man with a reputation for roistering and practical jokes. He was the hero of the countryside, famous for his feats of strength and horsemanship. Where Ichabod was lean and lanky, Brom was broad and muscular. Where Ichabod approached Katrina with formal manners and singing lessons, Brom swept her off her feet with boisterous charm and rustic gallantry.

One autumn evening, Ichabod was invited to a merrymaking at the Van Tassel homestead. The feast was abundant: roast meats, pies of all descriptions, doughnuts, sweetmeats, and flagons of cider. After the meal, the company gathered around the hearth to tell ghost stories. The favorite subject was the Headless Horseman of Sleepy Hollow, said to be the ghost of a Hessian trooper whose head had been carried away by a cannonball during the Revolutionary War. The specter was said to ride forth each night in search of his lost head, galloping through the woods with terrifying speed.

As the evening wore on, Ichabod grew increasingly uneasy. Brom Bones, seeing the schoolmaster's fear, told the most dreadful tale of all: that the Headless Horseman had been seen returning to the old churchyard each night, and that he could be outrun only by reaching the bridge that crossed the stream near the church. Once across the bridge, the horseman would vanish in a flash of fire.

Ichabod took his leave late that night, his imagination filled with the stories he had heard. He rode home on his borrowed plow horse, Gunpowder, a broken-down creature as gaunt and bony as Ichabod himself. As he passed through the dark woods, every shadow seemed to move, every sound seemed to be the footfall of a specter. When he reached the tree where Major Andre had been captured, his terror mounted.

Then he saw it: a great, dark shape on the road ahead. It was a horseman, huge and silent, wrapped in a cloak. Ichabod quickened his pace, but the figure followed. To his horror, the rider carried his head before him on the saddle. Ichabod drove his heels into Gunpowder's sides, and the horse plunged forward in a wild gallop. The Headless Horseman gave chase, his pursuing hoofbeats thundering behind.

Ichabod reached the bridge at last and spurred his horse across. He turned to look behind him, expecting to see the specter vanish. But instead, the Headless Horseman reared up and hurled his severed head directly at Ichabod. The head struck the schoolmaster with a terrible crash, knocking him from his horse.

The next morning, Ichabod Crane had vanished. His horse was found grazing at the gate of the Van Tassel farm, and near the bridge lay a shattered pumpkin. The schoolmaster was never seen in Sleepy Hollow again. Some said he had been carried off by the Headless Horseman, while others whispered that Brom Bones had engineered the whole affair. But whatever the truth, Ichabod Crane was never heard from, and the legend of Sleepy Hollow grew with each passing year.

title: Paul Bunyan
summary: In the early days of America, when the continent was covered with vast, untamed forests stretching from the Atlantic to the Pacific, there lived a lumberjack so enormous, so strong, and so skilled that he became the greatest hero the North Woods have ever known.
full_text:
In the early days of America, when the continent was covered with vast, untamed forests stretching from the Atlantic to the Pacific, there lived a lumberjack so enormous, so strong, and so skilled that he became the greatest hero the North Woods have ever known. His name was Paul Bunyan, and he was the king of the loggers, a giant among men, the inventor of the logging industry, and the creator of some of America's most famous landmarks. His feats are tall tales, of course, told around campfires by lumberjacks who loved to spin a good yarn, but they have become part of the very fabric of American folklore.

Paul Bunyan was born in the state of Maine, and from the very beginning, it was clear that he was no ordinary child. He was so large at birth that it took five giant storks to deliver him to his parents. When he was just a few weeks old, he could already lift a full-grown ox. As a baby, he was so restless that his parents had to float his cradle in the ocean off the coast of Maine so that the waves would rock him to sleep. One day, Paul started bouncing in his cradle, and his bouncing caused a seventy-foot tidal wave that washed away entire villages. His parents, realizing that the boy was too powerful for civilized life, moved deep into the Maine woods.

Paul grew quickly, eating fourteen cows' worth of milk each day. By the time he was a young man, he stood many feet tall, his beard was thick as a forest, and his appetite was legendary. He could clear an entire forest with a single swing of his ax. He was so fast that he once blew out a candle and jumped into bed before the room went dark. He was so strong that when he found a logging road too crooked for his liking, he simply tied one end of the road to a tree stump, hitched the other end to his ox, and pulled until the road straightened out.

It was during the Winter of the Blue Snow that Paul found his faithful companion, Babe the Blue Ox. One morning, after a night of strange blue snowfall that had painted the entire forest in shades of indigo, Paul went out to gather firewood and saw two ears sticking out of a snowdrift. He dug out what turned out to be a baby ox, frozen and near death. Paul carried it home and warmed it by the fire. The ox's hair remained blue—the color of the legendary snow—and Paul named him Babe. Babe grew at an astonishing rate. Every time Paul looked at him, he seemed to have grown two feet taller. Within a year, Babe was the size of a small house. His horns measured forty-two ax handles and a plug of chewing tobacco across. He could pull anything that had two ends. Paul and Babe became inseparable.

Together, Paul and Babe traveled across America, logging their way from Maine to the Pacific. Along the way, they created many of the nation's most famous landmarks. When Babe got thirsty, Paul dug out the Great Lakes to provide drinking water. When Babe got hungry, Paul cleared the forests of North and South Dakota, turning them into farmland. The Mississippi River was created when a water tank that Babe was pulling sprung a leak, and the water that spilled out carved a channel across the continent. The Grand Canyon was formed when Paul dragged his ax behind him while walking through Arizona. The Rocky Mountains were piled up from the rocks that Paul cleared from his logging sites. The Black Hills of South Dakota are said to be Babe's burial mound.

Paul's logging camp was as oversized as everything else in his life. The cook stove covered an acre. The hotcake griddle was so large that it had to be greased by men wearing sides of bacon on their feet, skating across the surface. Paul's crew included a cast of legendary characters: Ole the Big Swede, the blacksmith who could forge an anchor chain in a single day; Johnny Inkslinger, the bookkeeper who could write with seven pens at once; and Brimstone Bill, the only man who could handle Babe the Blue Ox.

Paul Bunyan's stories were first written down by W.B. Laughead in 1922 for a lumber company advertising pamphlet, but they had been told orally by lumberjacks for generations before that. The tales celebrate the American spirit of expansion, the courage of the frontier, and the humor of people who worked hard and played hard. Paul Bunyan may never have existed in reality, but in the hearts and imaginations of Americans, he is as real as the lakes and mountains he was said to create. He is the giant who cleared the way for a nation, the lumberjack who could do anything, the hero whose stories grow taller with every telling, just as Babe the Blue Ox grew larger with every look.

title: John Henry
summary: In the years after the Civil War, as America stretched its railroads across the continent, a new kind of hero emerged from the sweat and labor of the nation's working men.
full_text:
In the years after the Civil War, as America stretched its railroads across the continent, a new kind of hero emerged from the sweat and labor of the nation's working men. He was John Henry, a towering figure of African American folklore, a steel-driving man whose strength and spirit became the stuff of legend. His story is one of the most powerful and tragic in American folklore, a tale of human dignity versus the relentless march of industrial progress.

John Henry was born, according to the ballads, with a hammer in his hand. Some versions say he was born a slave in the 1840s or 1850s, others that he was a freedman working on the railroads after emancipation. What is certain is that he grew to be a giant of a man, standing six feet tall and weighing two hundred pounds at a time when most men were much smaller. He worked as a steel driver, one of the most dangerous and demanding jobs in railroad construction. The steel driver's task was to hammer a long steel drill into solid rock, creating holes for explosives that would blast tunnels through mountains. It was backbreaking work that required tremendous strength, endurance, and skill. A steel driver swung a nine-pound hammer all day, every day, striking the drill with perfect precision while his partner, the shaker, held and turned the bit.

John Henry was the best steel driver on the line. No one could swing a hammer faster or drive a drill deeper. He worked on the Chesapeake and Ohio Railroad during the construction of the Big Bend Tunnel through the Allegheny Mountains of West Virginia, a massive project that would enable trains to cross the rugged terrain. The work was brutal. Men died from rock falls, explosions, and a lung disease called silicosis—tunnel sickness—caused by breathing rock dust. But John Henry drove steel day after day, his hammer falling in a steady rhythm, his muscles rippling with every blow.

Then the company brought in a machine. The steam drill was a new invention, a mechanical marvel that could allegedly do the work of many men. The supervisors claimed it was faster and more efficient than any human steel driver. The workers gathered to watch the demonstration, fear in their hearts. If the machine could replace them, what would become of their jobs, their families, their dignity? It was then that John Henry stepped forward. "A man ain't nothin' but a man," he said. "But before I let that steam drill beat me down, I'll die with my hammer in my hand." He challenged the steam drill to a contest: man against machine.

The race was set. John Henry and his shaker would take one side of the tunnel, the steam drill the other. In a set amount of time, whoever drilled the deepest would be declared the winner. The workers gathered to watch, some cheering, others praying. The signal was given, and the contest began. The steam drill chugged and clanked, its iron bit biting into the rock with mechanical precision. But John Henry swung his hammer with a fury no machine could match. The steel rang against the drill, the sparks flew, and the sweat poured from his body. He drove his drill deeper and deeper, his hammer falling faster and faster.

At the end of the contest, the results were tallied. John Henry had drilled fourteen feet of solid granite. The steam drill had managed only nine. The man had beaten the machine. The workers erupted in cheers. But as the celebration began, John Henry staggered. His heart, strained beyond its limits, began to fail. He fell to his knees, his hammer still clutched in his hand. "I beat him, boys," he whispered. "I beat him." And then he died. They buried him near the tunnel, with his hammer by his side, so that even in death, he would be a steel-driving man.

The ballad of John Henry became one of the most widely sung folk songs in America, recorded by countless artists from Lead Belly to Johnny Cash. The story has been told in books, plays, films, and statues. It speaks to something deep in the American experience: the dignity of labor, the struggle of workers against the machines that replace them, and the heroic resistance of ordinary people against forces they cannot control. For African Americans, John Henry also represents a hero of quiet resistance, a black man who stood up to the white establishment and proved his worth, even at the cost of his life.

John Henry's story is not really about a man who died. It is about a man who lived with such passion and purpose that his life became a symbol. He represents all the workers whose sweat and toil built this nation, whose strength and determination created the railroads, the bridges, the tunnels that connected a continent. He is the hammer that falls in the darkness, the spark that flies in the tunnel, the heart that beats even when the body can no longer bear the strain. He is John Henry, the steel-driving man, and his hammer will never stop swinging as long as people tell his story.

title: Davy Crockett
summary: The legendary king of the wild frontier, folk hero of American expansion and tall-tale tradition.
full_text:
Born on August 17, 1786, in the wild hills of Greene County, Tennessee, David Crockett came into the world at a time when America itself was young and wild. His journey from a poor frontier family to the halls of Congress and ultimately to the walls of the Alamo is a story that blurs the line between history and legend, creating one of the most enduring American folk heroes of all time. Davy Crockett—the King of the Wild Frontier—was a real man whose life became wrapped in tall tales so extraordinary that even he struggled to separate fact from fiction.

Crockett's childhood was hard. He was the fifth of nine children born to John and Rebecca Crockett, who ran a tavern on the road from Knoxville to Abingdon, Virginia. At age twelve, his father hired him out to help drive a cattle herd to Virginia. When the job was done, the man tried to keep Davy by force, but the boy escaped, walking seven miles through knee-deep snow in two hours to get away. When he returned home, his father, angry at his truancy, was about to punish him. Davy, rather than face the beating, ran away from home entirely. He spent the next two and a half years wandering the frontier, working as a wagoner and day laborer, supporting himself by his own wits and strength. When he finally returned home at age fifteen, he had grown so tall that his family did not recognize him at first. He worked for a year to pay off his father's debts and earned his independence.

As a young man, Crockett became famous for his hunting. He claimed to have killed 105 bears in a single season, and while the number may have been exaggerated, there is no doubt that he was one of the finest marksmen on the frontier. He would track bears through the deepest forests, often alone for weeks at a time, emerging with enough meat and fur to feed and clothe his family for a year. It was this skill, combined with his gift for storytelling, that first brought him to public attention. He could spin a yarn that would keep listeners spellbound for hours, mixing truth with invention so skillfully that no one could tell where one ended and the other began.

Crockett's political career was as colorful as his hunting stories. He served in the Tennessee militia under Andrew Jackson during the Creek War, then went on to serve in the Tennessee legislature and the United States House of Representatives. In Congress, Crockett was a populist who fought for the rights of poor settlers against wealthy land speculators. He famously broke with President Andrew Jackson over the Indian Removal Act, a stance that cost him his political career but cemented his reputation as a man of principle. "Be always sure you're right, then go ahead" was his motto, and he lived by it.

The tall tales about Crockett grew faster than the man himself. The Crockett Almanacs, published from 1835 to 1856, were filled with outrageous stories of the frontiersman's adventures. They claimed he could run faster, jump higher, squat lower, dive deeper, stay under longer, and come out drier than any man in the whole country. They told of how he once grinned a raccoon out of a tree, his smile so powerful that the terrified animal simply surrendered. They claimed he saved the world by climbing to the top of a mountain and greasing the frozen axle of the earth with bear oil, then giving the sun such a push that it started moving again. He could ride a bolt of lightning, drink an entire river, and comb his hair with a pine tree.

The real Davy Crockett met his end at the Alamo on March 6, 1836, during the Texas Revolution. After a thirteen-day siege, Mexican forces under General Santa Anna stormed the mission. Crockett, along with the other defenders, fought to the last. Accounts differ on exactly how he died—some say he went down swinging his rifle like a club, others say he was captured and executed—but all agree that he faced death with the same courage and defiance that marked his entire life. He was forty-nine years old.

Crockett's death at the Alamo transformed him from a frontier politician into an American legend. The tall tales continued to grow after his death, and in the 1950s, a Disney television series introduced a new generation to the King of the Wild Frontier. The theme song, with its famous line "Davey—Davy Crockett, King of the Wild Frontier!" became a national phenomenon. Whether fighting bears in Tennessee, debating in Congress, or swinging his rifle at the Alamo, Davy Crockett embodies the American spirit: independent, courageous, humorous, and fiercely determined. He was part of the land, part of the dream, and his legend will never die.
